parts.content: Describe two methods for improving water use efficiency in agriculture.
parts.markscheme:
- Drip irrigation: Delivering water directly to plant roots, reducing evaporation <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Deficit irrigation: Applying less water than optimal during drought-tolerant growth stages <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Mulching: Covering soil to reduce evaporation and retain moisture <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Crop selection: Choosing drought-resistant or water-efficient crop varieties <marks>2 marks</marks>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the benefits of mixed land use in urban planning.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduced Travel Needs: Residents can access work, shopping, and leisure facilities locally <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Community Cohesion: Mixed land use fosters vibrant, diverse communities <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improved Quality of Life: Access to amenities increases residents' satisfaction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Environmental Benefits: Reduces car dependency, lowering emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Economic Opportunities: Increases business opportunities within neighborhoods <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conclusion: Mixed land use contributes to sustainable urban development and improved livability <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>6 marks max</marks>

questions.specification: The command term ""explain"" means students must give a detailed account including reasons or causes. Students need to define a dynamic ecosystem and provide a detailed account of how organisms in the soil influence the abiotic factors and interact with each other.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to explain soil as a dynamic ecosystem, which is directly covered under the concept of the soil system where biotic and abiotic components interact through transfers and transformations.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.1 Individuals, populations, communities and ecosystems > 2.1I Ecosystems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.1I Ecosystems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires an understanding and definition of an ecosystem (interaction of biotic and abiotic factors), specifically applied to the soil context.

parts.content: Explain why soil can be considered a dynamic ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Soil is an open system with constant changes in inputs/outputs and flows <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Soil contains a community of living organisms interacting with abiotic features <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Contains diverse organisms from microorganisms (bacteria, fungi) to larger organisms (worms, insects, moles) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Interactions between organisms establish complex food webs <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Organic matter is broken down by decomposers into simpler compounds that plants can absorb <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Compounds from plants are consumed by organisms, broken down and assimilated into new compounds <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Organic matter can be moved to lower horizons by organisms like earthworms/snails/slugs <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Movement of organic matter/organisms facilitates mineral cycling within soil and surroundings <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Physical features can change over time due to erosion/weathering processes <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Mineral cycling contributes to the dynamic/complex system <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award any 7 of the above points for maximum 7 marks</note>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.2F Agricultural choices and farming approaches (HL)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.2D Soil conservation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: A primary benefit of agroforestry is soil conservation, as tree roots stabilize soil and leaf litter contributes to organic matter, preventing erosion and maintaining fertility.

parts.content: Describe two ways in which agroforestry can contribute to sustainable land use.
parts.markscheme:
- Improves soil fertility through nitrogen fixation and leaf litter <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Provides habitat for beneficial insects and wildlife <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Reduces soil erosion and improves water retention <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Diversifies farm income through multiple products (e.g., timber, fruit, crops) <marks>2 marks</marks>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Explain one environmental implication of this trend.

parts.content: Explain the concept of "food miles" and its relevance to sustainable food systems.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each point, up to a maximum of <marks>3 marks</marks>:

- Food miles refer to the distance food travels from production to consumption
- Longer food miles generally imply higher energy use and carbon emissions
- Relevant for assessing the environmental impact of food choices
- Encourages consumption of local and seasonal produce

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1C Soil texture
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1C Soil texture
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to compare soils based on their sand and clay content, which is the primary focus of soil texture classification and how it affects basic soil behavior.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1G Soil properties (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1G Soil properties (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Comparing soil characteristics like drainage, aeration, and nutrient retention involves evaluating physical and chemical soil properties that result from different textures.

parts.content: Outline four differences between soils rich in sand and soils rich in clay.
parts.markscheme:
- Sandy soils have larger particle size / larger pore spaces; clay soils have smaller particle size / smaller pore spaces; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils have higher permeability / drain more quickly; clay soils have lower permeability / drain slowly and are prone to waterlogging; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils have lower water-holding capacity; clay soils have higher water-holding capacity; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils have lower nutrient retention / lower cation exchange capacity; clay soils have higher nutrient retention / higher cation exchange capacity due to greater surface area; <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Distinguish between management strategies that could improve agricultural productivity on sandy soils and those on clay-rich soils. Include a supported conclusion about which soil type is generally easier to manage for crop production.
parts.markscheme:
- Sandy soils: add organic matter (compost/manure) to increase water retention and nutrient-holding capacity; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils: apply fertilizers in smaller, more frequent doses to reduce leaching losses; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sandy soils: use mulching/ground cover to reduce evaporation and improve soil structure; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clay soils: improve drainage (e.g., drainage channels/tiles, raised beds) to reduce waterlogging; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clay soils: avoid cultivation when wet / reduce compaction (controlled traffic, appropriate timing) to maintain aeration and structure; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clay soils: incorporate organic matter/gypsum (where appropriate) to improve aggregation and workability; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supported conclusion: e.g., sandy soils often easier to work and drain but need more inputs for fertility/water, whereas clay soils retain nutrients/water but can be harder to cultivate due to compaction/waterlogging; clear judgement supported by preceding points; <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the extent to which soil texture (sand versus clay content) determines the sustainability of food production in an agricultural system.
parts.markscheme:
0 marks: The response is not relevant to the question or no standard reached.

1–3 marks: Limited statements about sandy or clay soils with minimal use of ESS terminology; little or no evaluation of sustainability; few links to food production; no or unclear conclusion.

4–6 marks: Some accurate knowledge of how texture affects water and nutrient cycling, erosion risk, and management inputs; some evaluation of sustainability (e.g., trade-offs between irrigation/fertiliser needs, runoff/waterlogging, greenhouse gas implications); includes at least one relevant example; conclusion present but limited support.

7–9 marks: Thorough, balanced evaluation showing how texture influences productivity and environmental impacts (e.g., leaching/eutrophication risk in sandy soils, runoff/erosion and waterlogging risks in clay soils, implications for irrigation, fertiliser use, and soil conservation); integrates multiple perspectives/HL lenses (ecological, economic, cultural/political) and shows clear links between points; uses well-explained examples; explicit, well-supported conclusion addressing the extent to which texture determines sustainability relative to other factors (e.g., climate, organic matter, management practices).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.2F Agricultural choices and farming approaches (HL)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.1F Human activities and selective forces (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Genetic modification is a significant human activity that acts as a selective force on the evolution and genetic diversity of agricultural species.

parts.content: Describe two ways in which genetic modification is used in agriculture and discuss one potential benefit and one concern.
parts.markscheme:
### Describing ways genetic modification is used in agriculture [2 marks]

- Genetically modifying crops to be resistant to insect pests/herbicides/diseases <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Genetically modifying crops to increase nutritional value/yield/tolerance to environmental conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Potential benefit [1 mark]

- Increased crop yields/reduced crop losses leading to increased food production <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Potential concern [1 mark]

- Potential negative impacts on biodiversity/ecosystems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ethical concerns about modifying organisms/unknown long-term effects <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potential for development of resistance in pests/weeds <marks>1 mark</marks>



<marks>4 marks max</marks>

<note>Maximum 1 mark for each part.</note>

parts.content: Using the figure, outline the trend in meat production from 2010 to 2017.
parts.markscheme: - Meat production shows an increasing trend from 2010 to 2017 in the regions included in the graph <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Using the figure, suggest reasons for the differences in meat production between the regions.
parts.markscheme:
- Cultural/religious factors can influence the types of meat produced/raised (e.g. avoidance of pork in Muslim/Jewish cultures; cattle considered sacred in Hindu culture) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Economic and infrastructure factors (e.g. increased wealth/investment; urbanization/cold-chain/transport improving access to inputs/markets) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Production and availability factors (e.g. lower production costs for poultry in some regions; high local beef production increasing availability) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>3 marks</marks> max
<note>Award marks for any three valid points from the categories above</note>

parts.content: Explain two global strategies to achieve sustainability in terrestrial food supplies.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following, <marks>2 marks</marks>:

- Production of food with extended shelf life to reduce food waste and associated emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Using GMOs with enhanced yields/heat tolerance to improve survival rates and reduce losses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reducing nitrogen losses to atmosphere to minimize climate change impacts and pollution <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Implementing low-methane rice varieties to reduce GHG emissions from this staple crop <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other reasonable strategies that correctly explain how they contribute to sustainable food supplies</note>

parts.content: Describe two methods of irrigation and their potential environmental impacts.
parts.markscheme:
<note>Award 2 marks for each correctly described method with impacts, up to 4 marks</note>

- Flood irrigation: Inundating fields with water; can lead to water waste and soil salinization <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Drip irrigation: Delivering water directly to plant roots; more efficient but can be costly to implement <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Sprinkler irrigation: Spraying water over crops; can lead to evaporation losses and soil erosion <marks>2 marks</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.2B Agriculture systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The Green Revolution is a fundamental example of the transition and characterization of agricultural systems, focusing on high-input, high-yield commercial farming.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.3A Sustainability and its pillars
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks for environmental and socio-economic impacts, which correspond directly to the pillars of sustainability (Environmental, Social, Economic) explored in this topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.2F Agricultural choices and farming approaches (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The Green Revolution represents a specific choice in farming approach (intensive industrial agriculture) that is compared against other methods in terms of its varied impacts.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.2E Diet and food security
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: A major socio-economic impact of the Green Revolution was its contribution to global food security and changes in human diets.

parts.content: Discuss the environmental and socio-economic impacts of the Green Revolution.
parts.markscheme:
### Environmental Impacts

- Biodiversity loss through widespread adoption of monocultures, as only high-yield crop varieties were prioritized <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Soil degradation resulted from intensive use of chemical fertilizers and pesticides <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Increased irrigation demands led to groundwater depletion in many agricultural regions <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Socio-Economic Impacts

- Significant increase in food production and crop yields helped feed growing populations <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Created economic disparities as wealthier farmers benefited more than small-scale farmers <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Agricultural mechanization reduced labor requirements, affecting rural employment opportunities <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>6 marks max</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts explicitly focus on contrasting and evaluating environmental value systems (Cornucopian vs Deep Ecologist; EVSs shaping movements; EVSs influencing agreement success).

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) concerns mitigating global plastic pollution, which is addressed via waste management approaches in ESS.

parts.content: Distinguish between the perspectives of a **Cornucopian** and a **Deep Ecologist** regarding the management of global freshwater scarcity.
parts.markscheme:
Distinguish between the perspectives of a **Cornucopian** and a **Deep Ecologist** regarding the management of global freshwater scarcity.

- **Cornucopian perspective:**
  - Believes that technological innovation will always provide solutions to resource depletion (e.g., large-scale desalination/wastewater recycling)<marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Views the environment as a resource to be exploited for economic growth/human benefit  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Deep Ecologist perspective:**
  - Argues that nature/water has intrinsic value regardless of its utility to humans<marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Advocates for a radical reduction in human consumption/population to live within the natural limits of the ecosystem  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not accept general definitions of EVSs that are not applied to the context of water scarcity. Comparison must be explicit or clearly implied to gain full marks.</note>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Significant historical events have played a crucial role in shaping the modern environmental movement. Outline how **three** named historical influences have shifted global environmental value systems (EVSs).
parts.markscheme:
Significant historical events have played a crucial role in shaping the modern environmental movement. Outline how **three** named historical influences have shifted global environmental value systems (EVSs).

- **Silent Spring by Rachel Carson (1962):** Raised public awareness about the dangers of chemical pesticides (DDT) / highlighted the concept of bioaccumulation / catalyzed the ban of certain toxins <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Bhopal Disaster (1984):** A gas leak in India that caused thousands of deaths / raised international concern regarding industrial safety, corporate responsibility, and the vulnerability of developing nations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Chernobyl Nuclear Disaster (1986):** A reactor meltdown in the USSR that released radioactive material across Europe / sparked a global debate on the safety of nuclear energy and the transboundary nature of pollution <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Minamata Disaster (1950s):** Mercury poisoning in Japan caused by industrial waste / demonstrated the link between industrial pollution and human health/neurological damage  <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Gaia Hypothesis (1970s):** James Lovelock’s idea that Earth is a self-regulating organism / encouraged a more holistic/ecocentric view of the planet <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for the name of the event and 1 mark for its specific impact on the environmental movement/EVS. Max 7 marks including 1 mark for clearly articulating the general shift from apathy to concern.</note>

parts.content: To what extent do different environmental value systems (EVSs) influence the success of international agreements aimed at mitigating global plastic pollution?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2B Negative feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) explicitly asks for the role of negative feedback mechanisms in maintaining stable equilibrium in a system.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2C Positive feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2C Positive feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) discusses the role of feedback mechanisms in pushing systems toward tipping points, which is the function of positive feedback loops.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2F Resilience of a system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2F Resilience of a system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) specifically mentions resilience and the transition of systems between states of equilibrium.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) is a detailed evaluation of the use of models, such as systems diagrams and simulations, in understanding ecosystems.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) requires knowledge of the soil system, specifically identifying transfers and transformations within it.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) requires understanding of the basic components of a system (storages and matter) applied to an aquatic context.

parts.content: Identify **two** major storages of matter that are present in a named aquatic ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Biomass (e.g., fish, plankton, aquatic plants) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dissolved nutrients (e.g., nitrates, phosphates in the water column) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Benthic sediments (e.g., organic matter or minerals on the lake/sea floor) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dissolved gases (e.g., oxygen, carbon dioxide) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The water body itself (as a storage of $$H_2O$$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each valid storage identified, up to 2 marks. Do not credit flows (e.g., precipitation or runoff) unless they are clearly defined as a storage within the context of the system.</note>

parts.content: State **one** transfer and **one** transformation of matter that occur within a soil system.
parts.markscheme:
- Transfer: Leaching (minerals moving downwards through soil profiles) / Runoff / Infiltration of water / Deposition of leaf litter <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transformation: Decomposition (organic matter turning into inorganic nutrients) / Weathering of parent rock / Nitrification / Denitrification <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks. Ensure one of each (transfer and transformation) is provided for full marks.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of models (such as systems diagrams or computer simulations) in understanding the complexities of an ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Models simplify complex systems, making them easier to visualize and study <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They allow scientists to predict future changes or the impacts of human activities (e.g., climate change models) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They serve as effective communication tools for explaining environmental issues to the public or policy-makers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They can identify gaps in current knowledge or data by highlighting where the model fails to match observations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, models are often over-simplified and may miss emergent properties or small-scale interactions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The accuracy of a model is limited by the quality of the input data ("garbage in, garbage out") <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models often cannot account for unpredictable, stochastic events such as natural disasters <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Predictions are often probabilistic and involve a high degree of uncertainty, which can be misinterpreted <marks>1 mark</marks>



<note>Accept any other reasonable evaluation points. </note>

parts.content:
Environmental systems are often characterized by their resilience and state of equilibrium. 

Discuss the role of feedback mechanisms in determining whether a system maintains a stable equilibrium or crosses a tipping point into a new state.

parts.content: With reference to a stage in Figure 3, describe one reason for a difference between food loss and waste in a less economically developed country (LEDC).
Discuss the sustainability of two solid domestic waste disposal strategies that can be used to manage food waste.
parts.markscheme:
<markscheme>
Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for each of two solid domestic waste disposal strategies discussed in terms of sustainability. Credit the economic, environmental and social dimensions of sustainability.

### Landfill
- Economic: Relatively cheap option in the short term <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Environmental: Produces greenhouse gas emissions/leachate that can contaminate soil and water sources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Social: Can lead to unpleasant odors/pests affecting local communities <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Incineration/combustion
- Economic: High capital costs for incinerator plant but can generate energy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Environmental: Releases air pollutants/greenhouse gases but reduces volume of waste <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Social: Potential health risks from emissions for local communities <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Composting
- Economic: Low cost and can produce useful compost product <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Environmental: Reduces waste volume and greenhouse gas emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Social: Compost can be used in community gardens/farms <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Anaerobic digestion
- Economic: High capital costs but can generate biogas as a revenue source <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Environmental: Reduces waste volume and greenhouse gas emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Social: Biogas can be used for cooking/heating in local communities <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Mark as a whole answer and give credit for other relevant points.</note>
</markscheme>

<marks>6 marks max</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.2 Energy and biomass in ecosystems > 2.2G Ecological Pyramids
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.2G Ecological Pyramids
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) uses ecological pyramids of energy as the specific structural context for explaining thermodynamic laws.

parts.content: Explain the relationship between the second law of thermodynamics and the increase in entropy within an ecological pyramid of energy.
parts.markscheme:
### Second Law of Thermodynamics and Entropy
- The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy (disorder) of a system increases over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In an ecological pyramid, energy is transformed from one trophic level to the next (e.g., from primary producers to herbivores)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These transformations are never $100\%$ efficient; a significant portion of energy is lost as low-quality heat to the surroundings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This loss of heat represents an increase in entropy, meaning energy is degraded and becomes unavailable to do work for the next trophic level  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare and contrast static equilibrium and steady-state equilibrium, providing a named example for each type.
parts.markscheme:
### Similarities
- Both represent states of balance within a system  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In both states, the overall system properties (observable state) appear to remain constant over a period of time <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Differences
- **Steady-state equilibrium:** Involves constant inputs and outputs of energy and matter, but the system remains in a constant state (e.g., a mature forest ecosystem or a living organism)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Static equilibrium:** No inputs or outputs of matter or energy occur, and there is no change in the system over time (e.g., a pile of rocks or a chair) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Steady-state equilibrium is characteristic of open systems, whereas static equilibrium is generally associated with non-living/isolated components  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Steady-state systems can return to equilibrium after a small disturbance through negative feedback, while a static system, once moved, establishes a new position  <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Examples
- Steady-state example: A pond (where water/nutrients enter and leave, but the pond remains the same)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Static example: A building or a geological formation (where no significant energy/matter throughput is required to maintain its structure)  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [4] max if no examples are provided or if only similarities/differences are discussed. For comparisons, ensure both sides are mentioned to earn the mark.</note>

parts.content: Discuss how positive feedback mechanisms can drive an ecosystem toward a tipping point, and evaluate the consequences using a named case study.
parts.markscheme:
### Content Guidance
Answers may include:

**Concepts of Feedback and Tipping Points:**
- Positive feedback loops are destabilizing; they amplify changes and drive a system away from its original equilibrium.
- A tipping point is a critical threshold where a small change can push a system into a completely new state/equilibrium.
- Once a tipping point is crossed, the change is often self-sustaining and difficult/impossible to reverse.

**Case Study: Arctic Ice-Albedo Effect:**
- **Mechanism:** Increased global temperatures melt sea ice $\implies$ darker ocean water is exposed $\implies$ albedo (reflectivity) decreases $\implies$ more solar radiation is absorbed $\implies$ temperatures rise further, melting more ice.
- **Consequences:** Loss of habitat for polar species (e.g., polar bears), alteration of global ocean currents (thermohaline circulation), and accelerated sea-level rise.

**Case Study: Eutrophication in a Lake:**
- **Mechanism:** Nutrient runoff leads to algal blooms $\implies$ algae die and decompose $\implies$ oxygen levels drop $\implies$ fish die $\implies$ more decomposition occurs, further stripping oxygen and releasing more nutrients from sediment.
- **Consequences:** Shift from a clear-water, biodiversity-rich state to a turbid, anaerobic, low-diversity state.

**Evaluation:**
- Discussion of the difficulty in predicting exact tipping points due to complex interactions.
- Analysis of the time lags involved in feedback loops.
- Consideration of the socio-economic impacts resulting from the shift to a new ecosystem state.

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below. |
| 1–3 | Minimal evidence of understanding positive feedback or tipping points. Fragmented statements with little or no link to a specific case study. |
| 4–6 | Sound understanding of feedback mechanisms. Includes a relevant case study with clear identification of the feedback loop and the resulting shift in equilibrium. Some analysis of consequences provided. |
| 7–9 | Substantial understanding of the relationship between positive feedback and tipping points. Effective use of a detailed case study. Thorough evaluation of the ecological and/or social consequences. Explicit judgments regarding the stability and irreversibility of the system. |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.4 Water pollution > 4.4A Water pollution
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4A Water pollution
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for a comparison between point source and non-point source pollution. These fundamental definitions of pollution types are typically introduced and assessed within the context of water pollution in the ESS syllabus.

parts.content: Evaluate the effectiveness of the three-level model of pollution management (Replace, Regulate, Restore) when applied to the issue of plastic waste in urban environments.
parts.markscheme:
### Understanding Concepts and Terminology
- Level 1: Altering human activity (Replace) - e.g., changing consumer behavior, bans on single-use plastics <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 2: Controlling release (Regulate) - e.g., legislation, recycling infrastructure, waste management taxes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 3: Clean-up and restoration (Restore) - e.g., ocean clean-ups, landfill remediation, removing microplastics from water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sustainability and long-term vs. short-term solutions <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Breadth in Addressing and Linking
- Level 1 effectiveness: Most sustainable, addresses root causes, but requires significant cultural shift/political will <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 2 effectiveness: Practical for immediate reductions, generates revenue for infrastructure, but can be bypassed or poorly enforced <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 3 effectiveness: Necessary for legacy pollution, but often expensive, inefficient, and does not stop the source <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Examples
- Specific mentions such as plastic bag bans in specific countries, the Great Pacific Garbage Patch clean-up efforts, or municipal recycling schemes <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Balanced Analysis
- Discussion of how the most effective approach usually requires a combination of all three levels (e.g., Level 1 is hardest to implement but most effective; Level 3 is easiest to visualize but least effective long-term) <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Conclusion
- A clear judgment supported by the preceding arguments. 
- Example: 'While clean-up efforts (Level 3) are highly publicized, they are ultimately futile without robust Level 1 and 2 interventions to stop the influx of plastic into the system.' <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>9 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the physical and chemical properties of Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) contribute to their biomagnification in arctic food webs.
parts.markscheme:
### Properties of POPs
- Chemical stability/persistence: POPs do not break down easily in the environment <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solubility: They are lipid-soluble (fat-soluble) rather than water-soluble <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High molecular weight prevents easy excretion from the body <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Transport Mechanism
- Long-range atmospheric transport (the 'grasshopper effect'): POPs volatilize in warm regions and condense in colder, polar regions <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Biomagnification Process
- Bioaccumulation: Pollutants build up within the tissues of a single organism over its lifetime <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomagnification: Concentration of the pollutant increases as it moves up through successive trophic levels <marks>1 mark</marks>
$$
\text{Plankton} \rightarrow \text{Fish} \rightarrow \text{Seal} \rightarrow \text{Polar Bear (Highest concentration)}
$$

### Impacts and Examples
- Specific mention of a pollutant (e.g., DDT, PCBs, or Dioxins) and its impact on apex predators (e.g., thinning eggshells, immune suppression, or reproductive failure) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>7 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Compare point source pollution and non-point source pollution, providing one specific example of each.
parts.markscheme:
### Point Source Pollution
- Definition: Pollution released from a single, clearly identifiable site or pipe <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characteristic: Usually easier to monitor, regulate, and control legally as the culprit is known <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example: A specific factory chimney emitting smoke or a sewage pipe discharging into a river <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Non-point Source Pollution
- Definition: Pollution released from numerous, widely dispersed origins (diffuse) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characteristic: Difficult to attribute to one source; requires regional or international management strategies <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example: Runoff of nitrates from agricultural fields or exhaust fumes from thousands of individual cars in a city <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 4 marks max. To achieve full marks, both types must be defined/characterized and both must have a valid example.</note>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Outline reasons for the changes in the forested areas of Africa over the period shown in the **Table**.
parts.markscheme:
- Deforestation due to logging/timber industry <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clearing of forests for agriculture/farming <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Urbanization/expansion of cities leading to loss of forests <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Forest fires (natural or human-caused) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Climate change/drought leading to loss of forests <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award a maximum of 2 marks. Accept any other reasonable response.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the change in the area of forest cover since 2005 may contribute to the mitigation of climate change.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- Increase in forest cover since 2005 leads to more trees/vegetation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Trees/vegetation absorb carbon dioxide (CO₂) from the atmosphere through photosynthesis <marks>1 mark</marks>
$$
\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{chlorophyll, sunlight}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{O}_2
$$
- This reduces the amount of CO₂ in the atmosphere, mitigating climate change/global warming <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award a maximum of 2 marks.</note>

### Method #2 

- Increase in forest cover since 2005 means more trees/vegetation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Trees/vegetation act as carbon sinks, storing carbon in their biomass <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, helping to mitigate climate change <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award a maximum of 2 marks.</note>

parts.content: Define the term "food security" and explain two factors that contribute to it.
parts.markscheme:
Food security: When all people, at all times, have physical and economic access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food <marks>1 mark</marks>

Factors: <marks>2 marks max</marks>

1. Availability of food (production and distribution) <marks>1 mark</marks>
2. Access to food (economic and physical) <marks>1 mark</marks>
3. Utilization of food (nutritional value and food safety) <marks>1 mark</marks>
4. Stability of food supply over time <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: To what extent are modern agricultural practices more effective than traditional practices in meeting the demands of the growing human population?
parts.markscheme:
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a general statement about the need to increase food production to meet the demands of a growing population
  - e.g. "With the world's population increasing, there is a need to produce more food"

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for describing how modern agricultural practices can increase food production
  - e.g. "Modern practices such as the use of fertilizers, pesticides, and high-yield crop varieties can increase crop yields"
  - "Mechanization and large-scale farming allow for more efficient cultivation of larger areas"

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for describing how traditional agricultural practices may be less effective in meeting food demands
  - e.g. "Traditional practices like crop rotation and organic farming may have lower yields"
  - "Smaller-scale traditional farming is less efficient and cannot meet the demands of a large population"

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for evaluating the sustainability and environmental impacts of modern and traditional practices
  - e.g. "However, modern practices can have negative environmental impacts like soil degradation and pollution"
  - "Traditional practices are often more sustainable and environmentally friendly"

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for a well-reasoned and balanced conclusion
  - e.g. "While modern practices can increase food production, a combination of modern and traditional techniques may be necessary to meet demands sustainably"

<marks>9 marks in total</marks>

questions.specification:
**Urban Agriculture in London**
* 9% of London's area is farmland, mostly in the outer green belt
* New focus on vertical farms and rooftop gardens with beekeeping

**Vertical Farming Details:**
* Located underground and in former industrial sites
* Grows salad greens, lettuce, spinach and herbs
* Uses 70% less water and no pesticides versus traditional farms

**London's Bees:**
* 1000+ beekeepers operate in the city
* Higher bee density than rural areas
* City bees are healthier with more diverse food options
* North London features an 11 km wildflower "bee corridor"

**Figure 1: Vertical farm**
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/bf35f22a-adce-4787-8797-1aeb0abf4697)


**Figure 2: Rooftop beekeeping**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c59311ba-4f2d-401b-bdc6-3dc15af96f0f)

parts.content: State **one** benefit and **one** drawback of the ecological footprint as a model for measuring sustainability.
parts.markscheme:
### Benefit of the ecological footprint model <marks>1 max</marks>

- Good visualizing tool/easily understood by non-specialists
- Environmental indicator/hazard warning indicator
- Widespread use, so easy to compare with other areas
- Highlights problem areas/identifies necessary changes in lifestyle
- Iconic symbol for raising awareness
- Information can be used to inform policy by governments

### Drawback of the ecological footprint model <marks>1 max</marks>

- Calculations are complex/all models are simplifications
- Needs huge amounts of data/uses proxy data/estimates
- Land has more than one function
- Less reliable at local level/not everyone in the region has the same lifestyle
- Can be interpreted differently by cultures
- Does not show types of resources used
- Does not include all factors that contribute to sustainability

<note>Do not accept only 'it is inaccurate/does not use outliers'. Do not accept 'does not account for changes over time'.</note>

parts.content: State **one** factor that would allow a region’s ecological footprint to exceed its biocapacity.
parts.markscheme:
- Importing resources/food
- Exporting waste products

<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Referencing both figures above, suggest how urban agriculture can increase London's carrying capacity
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following; <marks>3 marks max</marks>

- Increases food resources, (which increases carrying capacity) <marks>1</marks>
- Decreases water use, (which increases carrying capacity) <marks>1</marks>
- No pesticides, so less water pollution/does not harm/pollute environment / no pesticides used so fewer inputs/resources needed, (which increases carrying capacity) <marks>1</marks>
- Vertical farming uses less land area, (so more land area is available for housing) <marks>1</marks>
- Farming on roofs and in abandoned buildings means more land area is available (for housing/population growth) <marks>1</marks>
- Bees result in more pollination, so more primary productivity, (increasing carbon sink) <marks>1</marks>

<note>Do not accept only 'bees increase resources available' without reference to increasing food.</note>

<note>Do not accept 'bees provide healthier food'.</note>

<note>Do not accept only 'no pesticides used / provides goods / produces agricultural items / pesticides cause eutrophication'.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the humus content of soil affects its primary productivity.
parts.markscheme:
- Humus is rich in nutrients / essential for photosynthesis in plants and productivity <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Humus helps retain water in soil / increases water availability for producers / increases photosynthetic potential and productivity <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Humus enhances soil aeration / prevents waterlogging <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>2 marks max </marks>
